Religious Education
Teaching Module
vi
Preface
The Religious Education Teaching Module for form 1 has been developed in response to the 
introduction of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC), a transformative initiative aimed at 
enhancing quality education and relevance.
This teaching module is designed to support teachers to effectively deliver Religious Education 
lessons under the new curriculum. Its primary objectives are to, bridge the gap created by the 
absence of approved textbooks and equip teachers with practical skills and assessments tailored 
to learnersвҖҷ needs and levels.
The Ministry of Education acknowledges the challenges associated with transitioning to a new 
curriculum. Consequently, this module reflects our commitment to providing teachers with 
structured guidance, suggested activities, formative and summative assessments aligned with 
the CBCвҖҷs objectives.
While this module serves as a foundational resource, we encourage teachers to supplement it 
with their own research and innovations to address potential gaps during implementation. The 
suggested activities and assessments are designed to foster practical and critical thinking skills 
in learners, ensuring relevance and adaptability across diverse school contexts.
Teachers are encouraged to tailor suggestions to their unique classroom dynamics and explore 
alternative approaches when necessary. We hope this module will empower teachers to navigate 
the new competence-based curriculum with confidence, inspiring and equipping learners with 
applied knowledge, skills,В andВ values.
Kelvin Mambwe (Dr)
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
vii

expertise have enriched the content and quality of this teaching module.
We also acknowledge the dedication and hard work of the staff at the Curriculum Development 
Centre, whose tireless efforts ensured the successful completion of the teaching module 
development process.
Thank you all for your contributions.
Charles Ndakala (Dr.)
Director - Curriculum Development
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
ix
Introduction 
The Religious Education Teaching module aims to provide teachers with the tools, knowledge, 
and strategies needed to foster a deep understanding and appreciation of religious education 
among learners.
As teachers, you play an essential role in guiding young minds towards ethical and moral 
development, while nurturing their spiritual growth and cultural awareness. This module is 
designed to support you in creating a learning environment that encourages critical thinking, 
respectful dialogue, and a holistic exploration of religious beliefs and practices.
By engaging with the content and activities within this module, you will be equipped to, develop 
learnersвҖҷ competencies in understanding and analysing religious concepts, encourage empathy 
and respect for diverse religious perspectives, facilitate meaningful discussions that promote 
ethical reasoning and moral decision-making and create inclusive and supportive classroom 
environments that celebrate diversity and foster a sense of belonging.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
1
TOPIC: 1.3   MORALITY AND VALUE
Introduction
Morals are societal norms that dictate what is considered right and wrong within a community. 
Values, on the other hand, are guiding principles, standards or behaviour that are regarded as 
desirable or held in high esteem in society. Morality is a set of rules and beliefs that distinguish 
good behaviour from bad behaviour. Morality refers to the principles and values that guide 
our behaviour and decision-making, while values represent our deepest beliefs about what is 
important and desirable. Morality and values are essential in our daily lives because they shape 
our identity, guide our decision and impact our relationships.
Let us now embark on this thought provoking journey together, exploring the complexities of 
morality and values, and how they impact our lives and the world around us. 
GENERAL COMPETENCES
Learners should acquire the following competences:
Analytical Thinking
Examine sources of religious and secular moral codes
Critical Thinking
Differentiate sources of religious and secular moral codes
Emotional Intelligence 
Apply moral principles in daily living
Analyse interpersonal dilemmas
Problem Solving
Analyse moral dilemma
Hook
Imagine stealing medicine in order to save a dying person. Would that be right or wrong?
KEY  TERMS
TERM
MEANING
Moral
acceptable standards of behaviour
Morality 
is a set of rules and beliefs that distinguish good and bad behaviour
Amoral 
behaviour that is unconcerned with acceptable standards of behaviour
Immoral 
unacceptable standards of behaviour
Secular 
refers to something that is separate from or not connected to religion or spiritual 
beliefs
Dharma 
refers to many things : religion, law, duty, order, proper conduct, morality, 
righteousness or justice
Karma 
refers to the spiritual principle of cause and effect, where an individualвҖҷs actions, 
intentions and decisions influence their future experiences and circumstances
Caste 
is a social group that you are born into and it determines your position in life
Dilemma 
is a situation that requires a choice between two equal alternatives
Value
is a guiding principle, standard or behaviour that is regarded as desirable or held 
in high esteem in society
M o r a l 
code
a set of rules that are widely accepted by a group of people to show them what is 
right and wrong

Religious Education
Teaching Module
2
SUB-TOPIC 1.3.1 MORALITY
Introduction
Morality is a set of rules and beliefs that distinguish good behaviour from bad behaviour. These 
are principles and values that guide our behaviour and decision-making. Morality helps us 
distinguish right from wrong, good from bad and just from unjust. Morality is essential because 
it helps us make choices that respect the rights and dignity of others. By living morally, we 
contribute to creating a more just, compassionate, and peaceful world.
In this sub-topic, we will look at moral principles and discuss real-life situations that challenge 
our morals and principles. We will further explore effective strategies for making moral decisions 
and taking responsible actions.
Specific Competence: 1.3.1.1 Apply moral principles in daily living
Learning environment: Indoor or Outdoor (Natural /Artificial/Technological)
Teaching and Learning Materials
 
ВҲ Charts, posters and pictures showing different behaviour
 
ВҲ Projectors (videos on moral issues)
 
ВҲ Laptop, computer
 
ВҲ Longman Religious Education Grade 8
 
ВҲ Competence Based RE Syllabus 2024
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
ACTIVITY 1: 
Describing morality 
Learning environment
Artificial: indoor activities вҖ“ group work and classroom discussion
ACTIVITY PROCESS
 
ВҲ Cluster the learners in small groups to discuss the concept of morality
 
ВҲ Each group to report key ideas from the discussion to the class 
 
ВҲ Key ideas to be grouped and written down on the board
 
ВҲ Learners to connect the ideas and come up with the definition of morality
 
ВҲ Teacher to consolidate the meaning of morality on the board
CONTENT TIPS
 
ВҲ Morality is a set of rules and beliefs that distinguish good behaviour from bad 
behaviour.
 
ВҲ It deals with the way people should behave towards each other, animals and the 
environment.
 
ВҲ A system of morality guides acceptable behaviour, for example, in marriage, while 
rising and disciplining children and in business transactions.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
3
Moral, an Immoral and Amoral Person
 
ВҲ A moral person does what is right, and has a strong sense of what is right and 
wrong. 
 
ВҲ An immoral person is one who behaves in a way that is not morally acceptable.
 
ВҲ An amoral person is unconcerned with behaving in a morally acceptable way.
 
ВҲ The best known example of a moral principle is вҖңthe Golden RuleвҖқ or ethic of 
reciprocity. вҖңDo unto others as you would have them do unto you.вҖқ 
ACTIVITY 2: 
Discussing opinions on what is right and wrong
Learning environment
Artificial: Indoor activity 
 
ВҲ Discuss in groups
 
ВҲ Leaners to debate given motion
Technological
Virtual 
 
ВҲ Learners to watch different scenes on morality and debate on a given motion
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Learners to brain storm opinions on what is right or wrong
 
ВҲ Teacher to allow as many contributions as possible and write them on the board
 
ВҲ Class to select meaningful points
 
ВҲ Teacher to consolidates and summarises meaningful points on the board
Task 2
 
ВҲ Learners in groups to discuss opinions on what is right or wrong
 
ВҲ Group to share findings with the class
 
ВҲ Teacher to be write down information from the discussions
 
ВҲ Learners to role play opinions
 
ВҲ Teacher to critique work done by the groups
Task 3
 
ВҲ Teacher to choose a motion for the learners e.g. вҖң Abortion should be legalised in 
ZambiaвҖқ 
 
ВҲ Teacher divides learners into smaller groups and assign them roles
 
ВҲ Learners to be allowed to prepare their arguments
 
ВҲ As the learners debate, teacher to act as a moderator 
 
ВҲ After debate, teacher to summarise key points raised from the debate on the board
CONTENT TIPS
 
ВҲ Opinions on what is right and what is wrong vary widely among individuals and 
cultures. Factors influencing these opinions include: cultural norms, personal 
experiences, religious or spiritual beliefs that shape an individualвҖҷs perception of

Religious Education
Teaching Module
4
right and wrong.
 
ВҲ Right is typically associated with actions that are ethical, just, and beneficial to 
others, while wrong is linked to actions that are unethical, unjust, and harmful. 
 
ВҲ Making the distinction between right and wrong is essential for maintaining a 
harmonious society and upholding moral values. 
 
ВҲ While what is considered right or wrong can vary depending on cultural norms 
and personal beliefs, the fundamental principles of morality remain constant across 
different societies.  For example, the opinions on abortion vary widely, with some 
people believing it is morally wrong and others seeing it as necessary right.
 
ВҲ For example, eating cows is moral in Zambia but immoral in India. This allegedly 
shows that any act is right or wrong based on the values of the particular culture in 
which such an act occurs. 
 
ВҲ However, many different cultures do agree on certain universal moral values. For 
example, virtually all cultures agree thatВ torturingВ innocent people for fun is always 
immoral and that helping others in need is always moral.
 
ВҲ Ultimately, the choice between right and wrong lies in our hands and it is our 
responsibility to make ethical decisions that align with our values and principles. В 
ACTIVITY 3: 
Analysing the consequences of moral and immoral actions
Learning environment 
Artificial: Indoor activity 
 
ВҲ Learners answer oral questions
 
ВҲ Learners to role-play different scenarios of consequences
 
ВҲ Guest speaker 
Technology
 
ВҲ Learners watch videos on consequences of moral and immoral actions
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Learners to watch videos with different scenes of moral and immoral actions
 
ВҲ Teacher to ask questions based on the video
 
ВҲ Learners to discuss the questions arising from the video
 
ВҲ Teacher to consolidates the key points of the discussion on the board
Task 2
 
ВҲ Teacher to identify and invite a guest speaker (e.g. police officers, judge, guidance 
teachers вҖҰ) to give a talk on the consequences of  moral and immoral actions
 
ВҲ Learners to take note of the main points from the talk
 
ВҲ Learners to role play the consequences of moral and immoral actions
CONTENT TIPS
 
ВҲ The consequences of moral and immoral actions can have a profound impact on 
individuals and society as a whole.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
5
 
ВҲ Moral actions often lead to positive outcomes such as trust, respect, social harmony, 
fostering of integrity, accountability, responsibility, development and corporation 
 
ВҲ Immoral actions on the other hand can result in guilt and shame, loss of self-respect, 
strained relationships, community harm, social insecurity, loss of freedom, mental 
health, legal consequences such as imprisonment, mistrust and destruction of 
reputation.
ACTIVITY 4: 
Showing ways of applying moral principles in daily living
Learning environment
Artificial 
 
ВҲ Learners to discuss and role play
 
ВҲ Learners to interact with the Guest speaker
Natural: 
 
ВҲ Learners to research in the community
Technological 
 
ВҲ Learners to research on internet where possible
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Learners to research on ways of applying moral principles in daily living.
 
ВҲ Learners should be allowed access to the internet where possible.
 
ВҲ A guest speaker can also make a talk on how to apply moral principle in daily living.
 
ВҲ Learners to make a presentation of their research.
 
ВҲ Teacher to write meaningful points on the board
Task 2
 
ВҲ Cluster the learners in small groups to prepare for the role play
 
ВҲ Each group to role-play the given scenarios
 
ВҲ Explain the moral lesson of the scene at the end
 
ВҲ Class discussion to cement the lesson
CONTENT TIPS
Applying moral principles in daily living involves incorporating ethical values and 
principles into your thoughts, words and actions. Here are some ways to apply moral 
principles in daily living:
 
ВҲ Honesty and Integrity:  Practice Honesty: Always tell the truth, even when itвҖҷs 
difficult. This builds a reputation for reliability.
 
ВҲ Volunteering and community service:  contribute to the well-being of your community 
through volunteering and community service
 
ВҲ Active Listening and Open communication: Make an effort to understand othersвҖҹ 
perspectives by listening without interruption.

Religious Education
Teaching Module
6
 
ВҲ Empathy and Compassion: Engage in acts of kindness, like helping a neighbor or 
volunteering, to cultivate empathy in your community.
 
ВҲ Respect and Tolerance: treat others with respect, even if you disagree with their 
views or beliefs
 
ВҲ Forgiveness and reconciliation: practice forgiveness and work towards reconciliation 
in conflict
 
ВҲ Environmental Responsibility: take actions to reduce environmental impact and 
promote sustainability
 
ВҲ Accountability and responsibility: take ownership of your actions and its consequences
 
ВҲ Civic participation: Engage in civic duties, such as voting or participating in 
community service, to fulfill your role as a responsible citizen.
Expected Standard Moral principles applied in daily living accordingly
Specific Competence: 1.3.1.2 Analyse moral dilemmas
Learning environment: Indoor or Outdoor (Natural /Artificial/Technological)
Teaching and Learning Materials
 
ВҲ Charts, posters and pictures showing different behaviour
 
ВҲ Projectors (videos on moral issues)
 
ВҲ Laptop, computer
 
ВҲ Longman Religious Education Grade 8
 
ВҲ Competence Based Religious Education Syllabus 2023
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
ACTIVITY 1: 
Describing moral dilemma
Learning Environment
Artificial: indoor activities
 
ВҲ Learners brain storm the meaning of вҖҳmoral dilemmaвҖҷ
 
ВҲ Group work and presentations
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Learners to brain storm on the meaning of the phrase вҖҳmoral dilemmaвҖҷ
 
ВҲ Learners to present the findings in class
 
ВҲ Teacher to consolidate meaningful points and examples on the board
Task 2
 
ВҲ Teacher to cluster the learners in small groups and let them discuss the meaning of 
moral dilemma
 
ВҲ After the discussions, each group to make a presentation and share key ideas to the 
class
 
ВҲ Teacher to write down key responses on the board

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
7
CONTENT TIPS
 
ВҲ Moral dilemma is a situation that requires a choice between two equal alternatives. 
 
ВҲ Therefore, a moral dilemma is a situation where you have to choose between two 
conflicting moral and behaviour. 
 
ВҲ You cannot choose both behaviour, and inevitably you will fail to keep same aspects 
of your moral code.
ACTIVITY 2: 
Analysing moral dilemmas
Learning Environment
Artificial: Indoor activity 
 
ВҲ Group work on moral dilemma
Technological
 
ВҲ Learners to view pictures and watch videos 
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Teacher to divide learners into small groups to discuss moral dilemma
 
ВҲ After discussion, the groupsвҖҷ report findings to the class
 
ВҲ Teacher to summarise the information on the board
Task 2
 
ВҲ Teacher to displays images or shows videos with different scenarios of moral dilemma
 
ВҲ Learners to discuss the scenes in groups and share the findings 
 
ВҲ Teacher to makes a summary of meaningful points on the board
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Moral dilemma can manifest in various ways such as: 
 
ВҲ Conflicting obligations: Choosing between loyalty to a friend and honesty about 
their wrong doing.
 
ВҲ Competing values: Deciding whether to report unethical behaviour at work which 
could harm a colleague or not.
Analysing moral dilemma involves breaking down the situation. 
i.	 Define the dilemma: clearly define the situation in details, determine the central 
ethical issue or question at the heart of the dilemma
ii.	 Identify stakeholders and interest: identify those affected by the dilemma and 
understand the needs, desires and concerns of each of them
iii.	 Evaluate ethical principles and values: determine which ethical principles or 
values are relevant to the dilemma and weigh the importance of each value or 
principle in the context of the dilemma

Religious Education
Teaching Module
8
iv.	 Consider alternative course of action:  brainstorm potential solutions or course 
of action and assess the potential consequences, benefits and drawbacks of each 
option.
v.	 Choose a course of action: based on your analysis, choose the course of action that 
best aligns with your values and principles, explain why you chose this option.
vi.	 Reflect and review: consider the potential consequences of your action and be 
willing to revise your decision if new information becomes available.
ACTIVITY 3: 
Applying critical thinking in solving moral dilemmas
Learning Environment
 Artificial: indoor  
 
ВҲ Learners discuss various situations
 
ВҲ Learners role play scenarios depicting dilemmas
Natural: Outdoor
 
ВҲ Learners observe within the school situations that are conflicting
 
ВҲ Discuss in small groups their findings
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Teacher to instruct learners to conduct research on how to apply critical thinking in 
solving moral dilemmas
 
ВҲ Learners to use available resources in the school to conduct the research
 
ВҲ Learners to present their findings in class
 
ВҲ Teacher to consolidate and summarise the findings of the research on the board
Task 2
 
ВҲ Learners role play different moral dilemmas
 
ВҲ Class discusses solutions to different moral dilemmas
 
ВҲ Teachers to make a summary of meaningful points
CONTENT TIPS
Critical thinking involves systematically evaluating moral dilemma by:
a)	 Identifying the conflict: clearly define the competing moral principle
b)	 Gathering information: collect relevant information, facts and perspectives on the 
situation
c)	 Evaluating consequences: considering the potential outcomes of each option
d)	 Making a decision: choose the best action that aligns most closely with core 
values and ethical principles.
Example
Suppose youвҖҷre a nurse and discover that a doctor has made a serious medical error 
that could harm a patient. Do you report the error, potentially damaging the doctorвҖҷs

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
9
reputation, or keep quiet to protect the doctorвҖҷs career?
1. Define the dilemma: Report the error vs. protect the doctorвҖҷs reputation.
2. Identify stakeholders: Patient, doctor, hospital administration.
3. Evaluate principles: Patient safety, honesty, loyalty to colleagues.
4. Consider options: Report the error, discuss with the doctor, keep quiet.
5. Choose a course of action: Report the error to ensure patient safety.
6. Justify decision: Prioritize patient safety and honesty over loyalty to colleagues
Expected Standard: Moral dilemmas analyzed accordingly
SUB-TOPIC: 1. 3. 2 SOURCES OF MORAL CODE
Introduction
Morals differ among cultures and can be relative. Therefore, a moral code is dependent upon 
situation and context in which it is upheld. However, some morals seem to be universally true, for 
instance, it is wrong to murder or torture another person. Many morals originate from religious 
and secular (non-religious) sources. Examples of sources of religious and non- religious code are 
families, friends, school, communities, constitution and religion. We learn about morals from our 
families, friends, school, community, constitution and religion.
In this sub topic, you will discuss the sources of religious moral codes, identify secular sources 
of moral codes and evaluate religious and secular moral codes.
Specific Competence: 1.3.2.1 Differentiate sources of religious and secular moral codes
Learning environment: Indoor or Outdoor (Natural /Artificial/Technological)
Teaching and Learning Materials
 
ВҲ Charts, posters and pictures showing different behaviour
 
ВҲ Projectors (videos on moral issues)
 
ВҲ Laptop, computer
 
ВҲ Longman Religious Education Grade 8
 
ВҲ Competence Based Syllabus 2024
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
ACTIVITY 1:
 Identifying religious sources of moral codes
Learning Environment
Artificial: Indoor
 
ВҲ Learners to discuss the different religious sources of moral codes
 
ВҲ Learners listen to guest speaker
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1

Religious Education
Teaching Module
10
 
ВҲ Learners to read religious texts from different religious scriptures 
 
ВҲ Learners to present what they have read to the class
 
ВҲ Teacher to consolidate and summarise the sources of religious moral codes on the 
board. 
Task 2
 
ВҲ Put learners in small groups and let them discuss sources of moral code
 
ВҲ Each  group to make a presentation on what they have found to the class
 
ВҲ Teacher to write down key points on the board 
Task 3
 
ВҲ Teacher to invite guest speaker
 
ВҲ Learners to interact with the guest
 
ВҲ Learners to take note of the key points raised during the talk
 
ВҲ Class to discuss the points collected
 
ВҲ Teacher to wrap up the information from the points
CONTENT TIPS
Religious texts are an important source of moral codes. The moral teachings of the 
religious texts of Christianity, Islam and Hinduism are:
Christian religious text
 
ВҲ Christians believe that morals come from God or Jesus his messenger on earth. 
 
ВҲ They are revealed to us through the scriptures of the Bible.
 
ВҲ The Ten Commandments are a moral code found in the Old Testament according to 
Exodus 20:1-17. These were revealed to Moses by God.
 
ВҲ The New Testament contains moral teachings given by Jesus and his followers to 
the early Christians. Rom. 13:1-7, 8-14.
Hindu religious text
The Hindu religious texts include the Bhagavad Gita, the Vedas and the Upanishads. 
Each of these contributes to the Hindu understanding of morality.
 
ВҲ The Bhagavad Gita (the song of the Bhagavan) often commonly called the Gita is 
a 700 verse poem that is part of an epic known as the Mahabharata. It was written 
between 400BC and 400CE. 
 
ВҲ The poem tells the story of the Pandava prince, Arjuna, who is in a battle against his 
father. He turns to his Lord Krishna, who drives his chariot, for help and wisdom. 
 
ВҲ This is a very important poem in Hindu moral life. The battle field is interpreted as 
a metaphor for the spiritual and moral struggles that people have.
 
ВҲ It shows how our choices have consequences for ourselves and others.
 
ВҲ The Bhagavad Gita influenced the thinking of many visionaries in the 20th century 
including Dr Martin Luther King and Mahatma Gandhi.
 
ВҲ Gandhi called the Gita вҖңhis spiritual dictionaryвҖқ.
The Vedas 
The Vedas (meaning knowledge in Sanskrit) are a collection of many texts which come 
from ancient India. They are thought to be created by the Hindu god who created the

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
11
world Brahma. 
The Upanishads 
These are a collection of writings that teach people how to live a moral life
 Hindu morality focuses on the concepts of Dharma and Karma. 
i) Dharma can mean many things: religion, law, duty, order, proper conduct, morality, 
righteousness or justice. For example, in India there are different castes.
 
ВҲ A Caste is a social group that you are born into and it determines your position in 
life. There is the priestly caste (Brahmin), the traditionally warrior caste (Kshatriya), 
the traditionally merchant and farmer caste (Vaishya) and then the manual labourers 
(Shudra).
 
ВҲ The moral guidelines are to act in accordance with dharma, which means acting 
appropriately for your caste.
ii) Karma, on the other hand, has to do with cause and effect. Positive actions cause 
positive effects while negative action produce negative effects. 
 
ВҲ Hinduism believes in reincarnation, which means that the soul is reborn into another 
body many times.
 
ВҲ This karma can be collected over multiple lives, so Karma teaches that you act in a 
way that is as positive as possible.
Muslim religious texts
i) Quran
 
ВҲ The Quran is a sacred text of Islam which Muslims believe to be a revelation from 
God. 
 
ВҲ It was written by Prophet Muhammad, GodвҖҷs messenger on earth who said, вҖңI have 
been sent to perfect the moralsвҖқ. 
ii) Hadith
 
ВҲ The Hadith is the record of deeds and sayings of the prophet Muhammad. 
 
ВҲ They were collected, evaluated and written down during the 8th and 9th centuries 
and are still referred to in history and Islamic law.
 
ВҲ They are also used to interpret the Quran. For example, it is on the authority of 
Hazrat Anas that the Holy Prophet said, вҖңRespect your children. Teach them good 
morals.вҖқ
 
ВҲ The Quran guides Muslims in all issues of morality. The Five Pillars of Islam guide 
MuslimsвҖҷ behaviour. They are given in the famous hadith of Gabriel. These are:
i.	 Creed -Declare there is no god except God and Muhammad is GodвҖҷs messenger.
ii.	 Pray five times a day.
iii.	 Fasting- during the month of Ramadan.
iv.	 Alms giving- Give money to the poor (charity)
v.	 Pilgrimage --Travel to Mecca at least once in a lifetime.
Zambia Traditional Religion
 
ВҲ The elders will teach them how to behave, the cultural traditions through proverbs, 
songs, stories, myths and folk tales.

Religious Education
Teaching Module
12
 ACTIVITY 2: 
Identifying non- religious sources of moral codes
Learning Environment
Artificial: Indoor activity 
 
ВҲ Learners to discuss non-religious source
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Teachers to put learners in small groups to discuss non-religious sources
 
ВҲ Groups to make presentations on key ideas to the class
 
ВҲ Teacher to write key points on the board
CONTENT TIPS
Non-religious or secular sources of moral codes include:
a.	 Families: Families instill values and ethics from a young age.
b.	 Friends: peer influence can shape moral perspectives and behaviour.
c.	 School: Educational institutions teach principles of fairness, respect and 
responsibility.
d.	 Community: Local culture and traditions form collective moral codes.
e.	 Constitution: Legal framework establishes societal norms and ethical standards. 
Activity 3: Evaluating religious and secular moral codes
Learning Environment
Artificial: Indoor 
 
ВҲ Learners to discuss and evaluate moral codes
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Teacher to put learners in small groups to discuss the religious and non-religious 
(secular) moral codes
 
ВҲ Learners to make a presentation of key ideas to the class
 
ВҲ Learners to evaluate religious and  non-religious moral codes
 
ВҲ Teacher to make a summary of key points on the board  
Task 2
 
ВҲ Teacher to organise a panel discussion in which learners act as experts on religious 
and non-religious moral codes
 
ВҲ Learners to note down key points from the discussion
CONTENT TIPS
 
ВҲ Religious moral codes are believed to have been given by Supreme Being while 
secular moral code originates from society.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
13
 
ВҲ Religious moral codes are conservative while secular moral codes are more dynamic.
 
ВҲ Both religious and secular moral codes are influential and determine how people 
behave in society.
 
ВҲ Both religious and secular moral codes are rooted in long standing traditions and 
address traditional and contemporary issues.
Expected Standard: Sources of religious and secular moral codes differentiated 
accordingly
ASSESSMENT
Knowledge-based questions
1.	 Explain the meaning of morality.
2.	 Differentiate the following terms: moral, amoral and immoral.
Application-based questions
1.	 After losing his job, a man from one of the compounds in Lusaka decided to rob 
a shop in order to make ends meet. He was arrested and sentenced to five years in 
prison with hard labour. What would you have done in this situation if you were the 
man instead of robbing a shop?
2.	 Read the following moral dilemmas and discuss what you would do in each 
situation.
i.	
You find a bag of money hidden in a bush near your school. It has obviously 
been stolen from someone, but your family could certainly use the money.
ii.	
One of your good friends has discovered she is pregnant. If she has an abortion, 
she can carry on with her schooling and study teaching, which shows what she 
would like to do. However, she comes from a Christian family which believes 
that abortion is murder.
Critical thinking questions
1.	 Evaluate religious and non-religious of moral codes.
2.	 Examine the importance of morality in society.
SUMMARY
 
ВҲ Morality is a set of rules and beliefs that distinguishes good behaviour and bad 
behaviour 
 
ВҲ Opinions on what is right and wrong vary widely among individuals and cultures. 
 
ВҲ There are consequences for moral and immoral actions; consequences for moral 
actions are positive while those for immoral are negative.
 
ВҲ Application of moral principles in daily life in society is very important because it 
promotes harmonious living, peace and fosters good mental health.
 
ВҲ Moral dilemma is a situation that requires a choice between two equal but conflicting 
alternatives.
 
ВҲ Solving moral dilemmas requires application of critical thinking (identifying the 
conflict, gathering relevant information, evaluating consequences and making a 
decision)
 
ВҲ There are two main sources of moral codes, religious and secular (non-religious).
 
ВҲ Both religious and secular moral codes are important and influential in society.

Religious Education
Teaching Module
14
TOPIC: 1.4   CHOOSING AND TALENTS
Introduction
Choosing and talents are like the two sides of a coin as they cannot be separated.  They both 
play important roles in peopleвҖҷs lives. Life is full of choices, as a result people make choices 
throughout their life time. Some of the choices people make are good and others are bad, hence 
we have to follow all the necessary steps in order to make good choices. Choices can be made 
from chosen circumstances or circumstances that are beyond oneвҖҷs control. But in both cases, 
there are consequences one has to bear.
It is important to note that everybody has talents. A talent is a natural ability to do something well. 
Talents need to be discovered and developed through learning and practicing certain techniques. 
If well nurtured, talents may be a source of livelihood for a person. 
The topic will highlight information on how people make choices using specific steps at three 
levels which are individual, family and community. The topic will also focus on how a talent 
should be discovered, developed in order to promote entrepreneurship. The activities in the topic 
have been designed to promote practical demonstrations with a view that learners will develop 
competences that will help them in life.
In this topic, there are three sub-topics. These are Choices, Choices in the four religions in 
Zambia and Talents. From the presentations, teachers are expected to gain understanding and 
skills in order to present the topic effectively and confidently.
GENERAL COMPETENCES
Analytical Thinking
Analyse central teachings on making choices in the four 
religions in Zambia.
Citizenship
Explain responsibility for oneвҖҷs choices
Entrepreneurship
Develop talent to promote entrepreneurship
Financial Education
Apply financial literacy in order to develop the talent.
Hook
вҖңYour talents set the stage, but your choices steal the show.вҖқ
KEY  TERMS
TERM
MEANING
Inspire
To awaken something deep e.g. motivation in others
Talent
An ability to do something well
Possession
Owning something
Parable 
A short story in which a moral lesson is taught
Entrepreneur
A person who comes up  with a good business idea and then starts a 
business
Financial Literacy
Is the possession of skills, knowledge and behaviors that allow an 
individual to make informed decisions regarding money
Circumstance
A situation that happens in oneвҖҷs life. It can be chosen or un-chosen

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
15
Perseverance
The ability to continue to do something even though there are difficulties
Responsibility
A duty to behave the right way
SUB-TOPIC 1.4.1 MAKING CHOICES
Introduction
People all over the world share universal experiences and one of them is making choices. Life is 
a series of decisions we make and depending on our choices we must live with the result of that 
decision. This subtopic therefore, will explore how people make choices whether under chosen 
or un-chosen circumstances.
Specific Competence: 1.4.1.1 Accept responsibility for oneвҖҷs choice
Learning environment: Indoor or Outdoor (Natural /Artificial/Technological)
Teaching and Learning Materials
 
ВҲ Old Grade 8 Religious Education text books
 
ВҲ Models
 
ВҲ Projector / Laptop / Smart phones
 
ВҲ Charts/ pictures
 
ВҲ Competence Based Syllabus Religious Education 2024 
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
 
ACTIVITY 1: 
Describing how people make choices (see, judge and act)
Learning Environment 
Artificial: in door
 
ВҲ Class discussion on research findings
 
ВҲ Role play  and discussion on steps in making choices
Natural: Outdoor 
 
ВҲ Learners carrying out a research in the family and community 
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Learners to research on how elders in the family and community made choices in 
the past
 
ВҲ (Interview questions: Have you ever made any critical choice in life? What guided 
you in making those choices? Do you think any of those choices were wrong? What 
lessons did you learn from them?)
 
ВҲ Learners to report their findings to the class
 
ВҲ Learners to discuss each otherвҖҷs findings
 
ВҲ Teacher to summarise the findings on the board including the steps see, judge and 
act

Religious Education
Teaching Module
16
Task 2
 
ВҲ Learners to role play a scenario that depicts the steps followed in making choices 
(e.g. a pupil encouraging friends to smoke or drink alcohol)
 
ВҲ Learners to discuss the role play
 
ВҲ Teacher to re-emphasises the steps followed in making choices
CONTENT TIPS
There are three important steps that we need to follow in making good choices.
 
ВҲ See: we need to use our five senses to identify our options. Look at each option and 
think about it. 
 
ВҲ Judge:  In life, there are some things that are important (for example, passing 
an exam, playing a game, or starting a friendship).  Look at the advantages and 
disadvantages of each option. Evaluate the options and make the best choice 
according to interests, beliefs and also time that you have. 
 
ВҲ Act:  You take action according to what you saw and judged as the best thing to 
choose. 
ACTIVITY 2: 
Differentiating chosen and un-chosen circumstances 
Learning Environment
Artificial: indoor 
 
ВҲ Pair work on drawing and presenting tables on chosen and un-chosen circumstances
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Learners in pairs to draw a table in which they indicate chosen and un-chosen 
circumstances on the left and right, respectively.
 
ВҲ Pairs to present the finding to the class
 
ВҲ Learners to compare what other pairs have written and come up with a consolidated 
list.
 
ВҲ Teacher summarises the information into chosen and un-chosen circumstances
CONTENT TIPS
 
ВҲ Chosen circumstances are situations in life that you choose, e.g. the type of clothes 
you wear, friends you play with, food you eat.
 
ВҲ Un-chosen circumstances are situations in life that cannot be chosen, they are 
beyond your control e.g. your gender, parents, nationality.
ACTIVITY 3: 
Identifying the different levels of choices 
Learning Environment
Artificial: Indoor activity

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
17
 
ВҲ Group work and presentations of findings 
 
ВҲ Discussions on levels and factors that influence choices
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Learners in groups to discuss the different levels at which people make choices 
 
ВҲ Groups to report their findings to the class
 
ВҲ Teacher to summarise the information on the board
Task 2
 
ВҲ Learners in groups to identify factors that influence the decision making at any 
level. 
 
ВҲ Groups to report their findings to the class
 
ВҲ Teacher to summarise the information on the board
CONTENT TIPS
There are different levels of choices which include the following:
 
ВҲ Individual level вҖ“ making a choice for oneself, taking into consideration your needs 
and what to do in order to achieve them.
 
ВҲ Family level вҖ“ decisions made here have more than one personвҖҷs input because the 
choice has to take into consideration the familyвҖҷs common goals and what is best 
for the whole family.
 
ВҲ Community level вҖ“ at this level, many people are involved in making the choice; so 
a lot of people will be part of the decision making process.
There are many factors that influence choices. They include the following: age, gender, 
past experiences, level of commitment, existing beliefs, status in society, cognitive 
biases.
ACTIVITY 4: 
Role playing acceptance of responsibility of our choices
Learning Environment
Artificial: Indoor
 
ВҲ Learners discussing meaning of responsibility
 
ВҲ Learners role play acceptance of responsibility
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 
 
ВҲ Learners to discuss in groups the meaning of responsibility
 
ВҲ Groups report to the class their views
 
ВҲ Learners to role play various situations that demonstrate acceptance of responsibility 
of choices, e.g. a girl who falls pregnant and decides not to abort, a boy who gets 
drunk during school hours and accepts to be punished
 
ВҲ Teachers to emphasise on the need to accept responsibility of choices made

Religious Education
Teaching Module
18
CONTENT TIPS
 
ВҲ Responsibility is the duty to behave the right way or to do the right thing.
 
ВҲ It is important to accept responsibility of our choices whether good or bad.
 
ВҲ The choices that people make show oneвҖҷs aim in life (the type of friends you have, 
the way you use your free time, the way you treat people and even the way react 
when you are criticized)
 
ВҲ People should be confident and assertive in fulfilling their aims through the choices 
they make.
Expected Standard: Responsibility for oneвҖҷs choices accepted accordingly

Identifying steps of decision making 
 Learning Environment
Artificial: indoor 
 
ВҲ In groups, identify and discuss the steps involved in decision making process
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 
 
ВҲ Learners in groups identify and explain the steps involved in decision making 
process
 
ВҲ Learners present findings to the class
 
ВҲ Teacher write down the key points of the presentations
CONTENT TIPS
The following are the steps involved in decision making:
 
ВҲ See - examine the situation, identify the main problem
 
ВҲ Judge - decide what it is that you want to achieve, discuss what the most important 
area is, look at different options, decide what to do, decide how to do it
 
ВҲ Act- put the decision and plan into action
ACTIVITY 2: 
Evaluating decision making in real life
Learning Environment

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
19
Artificial: indoor 
 
ВҲ Group work and presentations on given situations
 
ВҲ Teacher assessment of group reports and giving feedback to the class
 
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 
 
ВҲ Learners to evaluate a given situation where steps for decision making are followed 
in arriving at the final decision and action
 
ВҲ Groups to present their findings inform of reports to the teacher
 
ВҲ Teacher assesses the report and discusses them with the learners
 
ВҲ Teacher to emphasise the importance of each step in decision making in life
CONTENT TIPS
The steps needed in decision making include the following:
 
ВҲ Examine the situation
 
ВҲ Decide what it is that you want to achieve
 
ВҲ Identify the main problem
 
ВҲ Discuss what the most important area is
 
ВҲ Look at different options
 
ВҲ Decide what to do
 
ВҲ Decide how to do it
 
ВҲ Put the plan into action
All the steps are necessarily and important in arriving at a good decision and action 
in real, everyday life. Failure to follow these steps may lead to bad decisions that will 
affect individual, family and community life negatively.
Expected Standard: Decision making evaluated accordingly
SUB-TOPIC:1.4.2. CENTRAL TEACHINGS ON MAKING 
CHOICES IN FOUR RELIGIONS IN ZAMBIA
Introduction
The teachings of a religion guide its followers in their daily choices. These teachings are important 
because they inspire people to look at their current life and the future with hope. People are 
expected to align or position their choices in line with the teachings of their religion. Therefore, 
peopleвҖҷs religious beliefs and values greatly influence their choices. 
In this sub-topic, we will look at what the four main religions in Zambia teach their followers to 
remember when making choices.
Specific Competence: 1.4.2.1 Analyse central teachings on making choices in the four 
religions
Learning environment: Indoor or Outdoor (Natural /Artificial/Technological)

Religious Education
Teaching Module
20
Teaching and Learning Materials
 
ВҲ Old Religious Education text books
 
ВҲ Bible, QurвҖҷan, Bhagavad Gita
 
ВҲ Charts
 
ВҲ Projector /Laptop / smart phones
LEARNING ACTIVITIES
ACTIVITY1: 
Identifying central teachings of the four religions in Zambia on making choices
Learning Environment
Artificial: Indoor 
 
ВҲ Group work and presentations on identified teachings
Natural: Outdoor 
 
ВҲ Learners carry out  library research or field research at Church, Hindu hall,  Mosque 
and community
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 1
 
ВҲ Learners in groups to identify the central teachings of the four religions in Zambia 
on making choice
 
ВҲ Groups to present and justify their findings to the whole class
 
ВҲ Teacher to write the summary on the board
Task 2
 
ВҲ Learners to do library research or outdoor research at a Church, Hindu hall, Mosque 
and traditional leader on religious teachings on making choices
 
ВҲ Learners to present their findings to the whole class
 
ВҲ Teachers to summarise the information on the board
CONTENT TIPS
The central teachings of the four main religions for followers to remember when making 
choices are as follows:
Christianity
Christianity teaches that follower must love God and neighbour (Mark 12:30-31). 
вҖҳNeighbourвҖҷ means your fellow man. Loving God means putting Him first in your 
life. This means that a Christian must use his or her time, money and other available 
resources in a way that pleases God.
Hinduism
The main aim of a Hindu is to be free from all selfishness. This is because Hindus 
believe that sin and suffering come about from peopleвҖҷs evil desires. The Bhagavad Gita 
says: вҖҳThe person who gives up all desires without thinking вҖҳmineвҖҷ or вҖҳIвҖҷ is the one who 
gains peace.вҖҷвҖҷ

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
21
Islam 
The main aim of a Muslim is to show complete obedience to Allah and to live at peace 
with every Muslim in the world. Muslims show complete obedience to Allah by following 
the Five Pillars of Islam (declare there is no god except God, and Muhammad is GodвҖҷs 
messenger, pray five times in a day, fast during the month of Ramadan, give money to 
the poor or charity and travel to Mecca at least once in a life time вҖ“ pilgrimage). By 
supporting each other in life, Muslims show that they are united and are at peace with 
other Muslims.
Zambia Traditional Religion
The main aim in the life of a follower of Zambian Traditional Religion is to show respect 
to fellow human beings and to be at peace with the spirits of the dead. Traditionalists 
are expected to show love to all people. In order to be at peace with the spirits of the 
ancestors, followers are expected to: give dead community members a decent burial, 
name children after their dead relatives, look after surviving members of their extended 
families, especially orphans, the aged and those who are differently able bodied and 
give gifts and offerings to the spirits of the dead.
ACTIVITY 2: 
Evaluating central teachings of the four religions in Zambia on making choices
Learning Environment
Artificial: indoor
 
ВҲ Group work and presentations
 
ВҲ Teacher summary and guidance
ACTIVITY PROCESS
Task 
 
ВҲ Learners in groups to analyse the central teaching by the four religions on making 
choices
 
ВҲ Groups to present what they have come up with
 
ВҲ Teacher to summarise the information and guide the learners on the relationship 
between central teachings and making choices
CONTENT TIPS
Christianity
 
ВҲ The central teaching of Christianity is to love God and your neighbor. 
 
ВҲ This means all the choices a Christian makes should be in line with this teaching. 
 
ВҲ Making choices in line with this teaching helps a Christian to be at peace with God 
and man to make progress in his or her life.
Zambia Traditional Religion
 
ВҲ The main aim of a Zambian Traditionalist is to be at peace with the spirits of the 
ancestors and show respect to fellow human beings. 
 
ВҲ Therefore, a traditionalist should make his or her choices in line with this teaching. 
 
ВҲ Doing so helps him or her to live a harmonious life with other members of society 
and remain faithful to his or her culture and traditions.

Religious Education
Teaching Module
22
Hinduism
 
ВҲ The main aim of a Hindu is to be free from all selfishness. 
 
ВҲ This is because Hindus believe that sin and suffering come about from peopleвҖҷs evil 
desires. 
 
ВҲ So Hindus should make choices according to this teaching. 
 
ВҲ Making choices in line with this teaching helps a Hindu to be at peace with Brahman 
and man to make progress in his or her life
Islam 
 
ВҲ The main aim of a Muslim is to show complete obedience to Allah and to live at 
peace with every Muslim in the world. 
 
ВҲ So a Muslim should make his or her choices according to this teaching. 
 
ВҲ Doing so helps him or her to succeed in their life.
Expected Standard: Central teachings on making choices on the four religions 
analysed accordingly
 SUB-TOPIC: 1.4.3   TALENTS
Introduction
We all have different talents and abilities, some of which we are born with while others are acquired 
through practice and experience. No matter what our talent is, it is important to develop it and 
use it to the best of our abilities. A talent is the natural ability to do something well. Everybody is 
good at something, but that something or talent needs to be discovered. For example, one person 
may have a talent for long distance running while another may have a talent for Mathematics. 
People have to try out many things in order to discover their special talents
In this sub-topic, we will discuss talents and how they can be discovered, developed and used to 
earn a living.
Specific Competence: 1.4.3.1 Use talent as a source of earning a living
Learning environment
Indoor or Outdoor (Natural /Artificial/Technological)
Teaching and Learning materials

ВҲ Models 
 
ВҲ Projector, Laptop, smartphones
 
ВҲ Chart
 
ВҲ Old Religious Education text books
 
ВҲ Competence Based Religious Education Syllabus 2024

CONTENT TIPS
 
ВҲ All talents can be developed into entrepreneurial ventures. For example, a person 
good at cooking can develop this talent into a catering business; a footballer can 
develop the talent into professional football etc.
 
ВҲ By developing talent into an entrepreneurial venture, a person not only earns a 
living but also employs other people.
 
ВҲ Developing talent into an entrepreneurial activity is one way of poverty reduction 
in society.
 
ВҲ However, for a talent to be developed into an entrepreneurship, a lot of skills and 
knowledge is required such as financial literacy, collaboration, consultation and 
availability.
 
ВҲ One also requires attitude of hard work, financial discipline, patience and inner 
motivation to keep on doing the work.
ASSESSMENT
Knowledge-based questions
1.	 Explain the three steps that people need to take in order to make a good choice.
2.	 From what we have learnt on вҖңChoosing and TalentsвҖқ, distinguish between talent 
and gift.
Application-based questions
1.	 Mr Lika graduated as a Religious Education teacher and was posted to Pimpe 
Secondary School. While at the college, Mr Lika belonged to the entertainment 
committee and at times, he would play comedy.  When he joined the school, his local 
community identified his talent of entertaining people especially during weddings. 
As a result, during weekends, he would be hired to be Master of Ceremonies. Mr 
Lika became well known in the local community and he would make good money 
for every performance that he did.
What advice would you give Mr Lika concerning his talent as an entertainer?
Critical thinking questions
1.	 Explain why it is important to take acceptance of responsibility of oneвҖҷs choices in 
life.
2.	 Explain in your own words, how a talent can promote entrepreneurship in oneвҖҷs life
SUMMARY
Key Points 
 
ВҲ People have a choice about some circumstances in their lives, such as friends, 
marriage partner and career.
 
ВҲ People do not have a choice about other circumstances in their lives, such as who 
their parents, gender and race.
 
ВҲ People can make choices as individuals, but sometimes they have to make choices 
that affect them as a group of individuals such as a family or community.
 
ВҲ When people make choices, they should follow the three steps: see, judge and act.

Religious Education
Teaching Module
28
 
ВҲ Religions teach their followers on what to consider when making choices.
 
ВҲ Christianity: Love God and love your neighbour.
 
ВҲ Zambian Traditional Religion: be at peace with both ancestral spirits and fellow 
people, and respect everyone in the community.
 
ВҲ Islam: show complete obedience to Allah and to live at peace with every Moslem 
in the world.
 
ВҲ Hinduism: to be free from all selfishness and desires.
 
ВҲ OneвҖҷs main aim in life can be seen in all the choices they make.
 
ВҲ A talent is the ability to do something well.
 
ВҲ A talent should be discovered and developed by an individual.
 
ВҲ An entrepreneur is a person who comes up with a business idea and starts a business.
 
ВҲ Financial literacy is the education one acquires in order to understand financial 
issues such as budgeting, investing, savings, communication and inter-personal 
relationships and record keeping.
 
ВҲ A talent can lead to entrepreneurship.  Most people start by identifying their skills 
or talents and go further to develop their talents into entrepreneurship ventures.

SUBJECT: Religious Education	
	
	
	
TOPIC: 1.3: Morality and Values (This must come from the syllabus with its serial 
number)	
SUB-TOPIC: 1.3.1: Morality (This must come from the syllabus with its serial 
number)	
GENERAL COMPETENCES: Critical Thinking, Emotional Intelligent, Problem Solving and 
Analytical Thinking
SPECIFIC COMPETENCES: 1.3.1.1. Apply moral principles in daily living (This must come 
from the syllabus with its serial number)	
LESSON GOAL: Describing morality	
RATIONALE: This lesson is on Morality and is particularly focused on describing morality. 
Through simulation, learners will develop competencies such as critical thinking, Emotional 
intelligence, analytical thinking and problem solving by applying moral principles in daily life. 
This is Lesson 1 of 3 on this sub-topic.
PRIOR KNOWLEDGE: Purpose of learning Religious Education (previous materials/
information learnt which is related to the topic at hand)

z Longman Religious Education Grade 8 
 
z Competence Based RE Syllabus 2024

Artificial Environment: This is a classroom-based activity
Natural Environment: This is an outside based activity
Technological Environment: The use of Computer and projector for pictures and videos.
TEACHING AND LEARNING MATERIALS/RESOURCES
Pictures, charts or videos showing different behaviour
EXPECTED STANDARD: Moral principles applied in daily living accordingly (This must 
come from the syllabus as it is)
